Yu Garden, located at No
132, Anrenfang Street in the center of the Old City, is regarded as one of the most lavish and finest Chinese gardens
The garden was constructed in 1559 of Ming Dynasty as the private garden by Pan Yunduan who spent almost 20 years to build the garden to make his parents happy
The garden is separated into four sections including the western, eastern, central and the internal areas
Sansui Hall in the western section was built in 11760 and was originally used to entertain the guests
Later, it turned into ceremonial hall for the gentlemen and bookmen
Exquisite Jade Rock is the real treasure in Yu Garden and also one of the famous rocks along the Yangtze River
3 meters high and has 72 holes
The wonder of the rock lie in the smoke can float from all holes when you burn a joss stick below the rock

Tips on visiting Yellow Mountain, Hongcun and Xidi
We don’t normally post such long posts but felt this might help others planning a trip to Yellow Mountain
Below is also info on Hongcun and Xidi
Yellow MountainWe did yellow mountain without a guide and it was easy
From the Crowne Plaza we took a taxi to the town at the base of the Mountain (called Tangkou) the hotel pre-arranged the taxi for us at a negotiated price (180 Yuan)
The trip took about 45 mins
The taxi drops you at the Passenger Distribution Centre
At the centre there is a ticket office where you buy a shuttle bus ticket (20 RMB) to get you to the area where the cable car and walking trail starts
(The hotel could not book this bus ticket)
You have to decide if you want to go to Yungu or Yuping cable cars
We chose Yungu because it is closer to the Beihai Hotel where we were staying on the mountain
When you have your shuttle ticket you proceed to the bus departure area
Here you are confronted by two lane choices
The left lane is for Yungu though the sign says Valley of the Clouds! Show your ticket and hop on one of the large blue coaches
The ride to the cable car station is about 15 minutes
Once here you can buy your park pass (190 RMB) and the cable car ticket (80 RMB)
We bought our park and cable car ticket at our hotel before we left but that isn’t necessary as there wasn’t a line for those
However, there was an enormous line to get onto the cable car
We got to the base of cable car at about 9 and were boarding by 11
We did notice that the queue was greatly reduced for those arriving at about 10
Most folk are day trippers who need to get up the mountain early
If you are staying overnight then we suggest arriving a little later around 10
After walking from the cable car stop via some amazing sights we went to Beihai hotel (about 30 min walk)
After check-in we did the Xiamen Canyon walk
Be sure to get a map from the hotel
The walk took about 4 hours and was beautiful
Quite strenuous! We are very fit - and our calves were still burning a couple of days later
The next day we retuned via Yungu cable car at around 10 am without any line up
Then it was the shuttle back to the passenger distribution centre
We wanted to take the public bus back to the Cowne Plaza in Tunxi but that proved too difficult - while we found buses parked we couldn’t find anyone to help us work out what was going on
They charged 200rmb plus 20 for the toll
They originally quoted 240 but we haggled
Be sure to have your hotel’s name and address in mandarin!Hongcun and XidiThese two places are well worth a visit
Each has a different atmosphere
While both have tourist shops, Xidi has more beautiful handicrafts
The old buildings in both places have signs on them telling you what you are looking at so you can still have a good experience without a guide
The Crowne plaza organised a taxi driver to pick us up from the station (we arrived at 10am
) Our driver took us straight to Hongcun
The lovely taxi driver, who couldn’t speak a word of English, parked his cab and waited while we wondered around for about 2 hours
He then took us to Xidi where we did the same
We felt completely safe leaving all our luggage in the cab with him
He then took us to the Crowne plaza at about 4pm
The driver was 480 rmb for the day which we needed to pay in cash
Compared to the prices we were quoted for a guided tour of these areas this was a bargain
It & APOs; s beautiful
You should either go to the hotel bar and enjoy the view or dine in one of the restaurants

Our group of 10 people, most of us in our 60's, took a 4 day 3 night tour of Huangshan (sometimes called Yellow Mountains, which is a literal, but wrong translation of its Chinese name)
We arranged our tour with John of Huangshan, who is one of the best tour operators I have ever worked with
We spent one night in Huangshan City at the Crowne Plaza, and two nights at Xihai Hotel at the mountain top
John is extremely knowledgeable of the area
He arranged to pick us up at the Hangzhou Airport, and dropped us off at the Huangshan Airport at the end of our trip for our flight to Shanghai
He provided a very roomy and comfortable bus for us with an excellent driver, and a very enthusiastic guide who stayed with us the entire trip
I cannot recommend John enough, and you can find other reviews of his service on this forum
When we got to the mountain top, we were a bit disappointed because it was very foggy with intermittent drizzles
The first day we were not able to do much hiking
The next day was a different story
It was misty in the morning, but in the afternoon, the skies cleared, and we had a beautiful view of the mountains everywhere we hiked
We were very lucky to see the Sea of Clouds, and it was almost a religious experience
Between Xihai Hotel and the Xihai canyon there were very few visitors, and we had the sceneries almost all to ourselves
Absolutely amazing views of this most famous mountain in China
We mostly ate at Xihai hotel, and we ordered from their menu, so the food was very good
Breakfast was also very acceptable
This is the only 5 star hotel at the mountain top, and John booked us 5 rooms with balconies in the new wing
Very comfortable rooms with impressive views
Highly recommend that you stay at this hotel! On the 4th day, we came down and visited Hongcun Village, made famous by the movie "Crouching Tiger, Hidden Dragon"
It was a Saturday, and the place was very crowded
Frankly, it was a tourist trap, and could be safely skipped
Overall we had a great trip
It is important to avoid weekends and holidays when you visit Huangshan
We went in late October, and there was no waiting anywhere, and we only saw crowds early in the morning, on their way to see the sunrise
Otherwise, there was very easy access on all the hiking paths
By the way, Huangshan is named after Huangdi, the first mythical king of the Middle Kingdom who ascended to the heavens from this mountain
Back then, it was a very small kingdom

We arrived when it had rained for 2 days
Our afternoon was washed and misted out
It was miserably cold and wet
The next morning we woke up to see that it had snowed overnight, and we dressed warmly to go out and see the sunrise
It was a spectacular few seconds
However the light of the morning sun made good colourful snow scenery and we had many "salon quality" photos of ice and frost, on trees and rocks
After breakfast we hiked towards the cable car station on the western side of the scenic area (park)
The overnight snow and ice on steps made it quite a treacherous trek
We had to be triply careful to avoid slips or falls
Some other hikers wore crampons
Along the way there were many opportunities for very photogenic photos
It was no "walk in the park" though
We must have climbed up and down a three to four thousand steps along the short 8-10km walk (measured on a flat map)
Directional and informational signages can be much improved
Take along your own food and drinks as these are scarce along the way
The paths, and the many thousand of steps were paved with large stones slabs, dressed and set to perfection
I salute the workers who had to do this without the aid of heavy machines
Local tour groups can be noisy with their tour guides shout merrily and incessantly through their loud speakers

These should be banned on the mountain paths, but how else could these highly excitable, and perhaps tired and irritable tourists be kept in line?Compared to our trek in to the "Tiger's Nest" in Bhutan
I would say this was perhaps one and a half times more difficult
The day's icy conditions probably made it feel 3 times more difficult
There are a lot of people on weekends

Before : check the weather and if possible get there when it's good, plenty of websites for that, just do your howework, but if your dates are already fixed then like us just pray that the wind will move the clouds for you to see the scenery (which is amazing by the way), also buy water and food in a supermarket before climbing up (like all mountains in the world, everything up is more expensive), book not a room as prices are insanely huge comparing to "normal" China, book a bed in a dorm, all the 4 stars hotel up there have dorms, they are expensive, around 150-200 (and that's if you book through a chinese speaking person like we did, it can be more) but it's the cheapest solution to spend the night in the summit area

Then you're all set!Going there & getting in : bus from Tunxi is around 20, then normally they drop you at the bus station of the mountain, you NEED to take another bus around 20 again to get to the bottom and you need to chose which way to go up, our advice take the Eastern Steps (Cloud Valley), you can take the Western steps (mercy light temple) but it's so hard that it's better we think to do them on the way back, then at the eastern steps 2 choices, climbing the stairs or the cable car, hard or easy way, honestly if you're not very old or with a condition, the stairs are the best option, it will save you some money, you'll see some good stuff and it's just 2 hours and a half (NOT a day like I read in some previous comment), then you'll have to pay the huge entrance price, this is the only expensive thing we paid in Huangshan honest, buy a map also, quite useful and cheapOn the summit : with your map and the ones displayed frequently you should find your way easy but please have in mind that the maps you find on the way are displayed from the point of view of where you are standing, there is no North, South, etc orientation, and also names can differ a bit (if it looks almost the same then it probably is, to be sure check the chinese caracters), when you understand this logic this is really simple
There's plenty of food stalls to buy stuff, if you go to eat in the hotels of course it will be expensive
Unfortunately for us it was really foggy the first day, we couldn't see a thing
But Fairy bridge is nice, and Beginning to Believe looked good too (we could only imagine the view)
We didn't go to the sunrise as it was obvious that it will be packed but the Bright Summit, Central Sea Pavillion and Cooling Terrace are the places to see it
The second day was perfect, sunny and enough clouds to be just above them with the sun on all the peaks (so good after the previous day!)
We hiked a bit on the summit, then start our way down

Going down : cable or walking, of course walking :) Western steps are really hard even when going down, very heavy on the knees, but the scenery is really gorgeous with good weather (and that's what we had), it should be the best way to get out the mountain

After almost 4 hours you're at the bottom, same story than before, 1 bus for reaching the mountain bus station, another one to Tunxi, 20 eachOne word about the people : you won't find many westerners at the top, mostly chinese people and lot of them in tour groups
So yes, it's gonna be noisy with tour group guides shouting in megaphones, with the usual chinese spitting and way of speaking so loud, it's gonna be crowded, there will be some rudeness, blablabla
hey wake up you're in China! Not the same manners, not the same culture
One good way to avoid that, go off season, you won't feel that packed and you will enjoy the place (at least more)
Conclusion : we really enjoyed our trip at Huangshan but it's thanks to the weather on the second day, if it was foggy all the time, we wouldn't have probably
It was late march and not so busy, even on the eastern steps

On one of the banks of the river Huangpu runs this embankment / embankment known as Bund and faces on the other side of the river compared to the Shanghai Financial Center; under the embankment there are banks and other important offices in the thirties style
I Don't want to be sacrilegious, but the first thing that came to mind when I walked on the Bund was: here's the Shanghai "extrusion"
In fact, many of the visitors were made up of young couples, especially Chinese, of course

From the Bund you can enjoy an excellent view of the modern buildings and skyscrapers that are the heart of the shopping center (Pudong), and in particular the TV Tower (Pearl of the East), which is the highest and most modern in Pudong, in fact

Under the embankment, on the side where I was, there is also a bronze sculpture of a bull, which is the symbol of the Shanghai stock exchange; it is read on a bronze tombstone next to the bull: "the Bund bull is especially designed for the financial square of the Bund
It was designed by the famous sculptor Arturo Ugo Di Modica, who also made the bull that is located in Wall steer"

The difference that I noticed between the two bulls was that this one from Shanghai has very shiny horns and snout (these are the most touched parts by visitors), while the one in New York the clearest parts are genitals!

I must say that I would have preferred to walk in the evening but for lack of time I was there during the day and it was also raining! Seeing the city with all the skyscrapers and the TV tower all colored where beside it has balls that look like lollipops was fun and also spectacular

The Pingjiang district is one of the most historic in Suzhou and indeed in all of China
The tourist part of Pingjiang Street runs along one side of a canal between Bai Ta Dong Lu and the Ganjiang Canal and Road
It takes about half an hour to walk from one end to the other, but much more if you stop at some of the dozens of shops and restaurants
Suzhou is generally ill equipped for foreigners who do not speak Chinese

I speak and read a fair amount and even I find the lack of a map in pinyin and of a comprehensive guide in English very daunting, in addition to the fact that most staff in hotels seem to speak nothing but Chinese, and that often in a heavy regional accent
Nevertheless, there are signs in this street, and even a map or two, with translations in English and French
After having travelled for many years in many parts of China, I still find Suzhou however to be one of its most attractive cities
It has lost much of its beauty and character, but it retains far more than most of the others
And Panjiang Road encapsulates much of the refinement, the elegance and the mystery that many visitors to China seek in vain in say Shanghai, only half an hour away
Almost all the shops sell handicrafts, clothes and other items of interest to visitors or indeed sinophiles, and almost none sell cheap souvenirs
Some boast the handiwork of highly honoured artists

The prices can be high but often they are lower than elsewhere in Suzhou, especially near the Humble Administrator's Garden, and the quality of the silk, the pottery, the wood carvings and the paintings is generally extremely high
There are good hotels and youth hostels either facing the street or in lanes issuing from it
There are at least three shops selling extremely traditional Suzhou pastries, which are famous all over China
I dined at the Fish and Rice Epic Restaurant, which serves very authentic and delicious local dishes very cheaply
It has a pleasant atmosphere and staff and a menu in English and Chinese with photographs of the dishes in colour
I recommend this street to any visitor seeking the flavour of traditional China and Suzhou without most of the tedium of negotiating through the majority of Chinese streets
Although scooters and bicycles are a nuisance, there is no vehicular traffic and especially in the evening there is almost a hush that renders the atmosphere magical
This is not a theme park
Nearly all the buildings are old and many are ancient and magnificent, especially in the lanes extending from the street
The street is full of relaxed locals as well as tourists, and it is contains much to attract almost anyone, from children to the old and of any level of knowledge of Chinese life and culture
It is particular suitable for visitors who want to make the most of their time in a few hours, as one can sample there almost everything that Suzhou is famous for
There is even  the Couple's Garden near by
